source_paper_title: Invariant States of Hydrodynamic Limits of Randomized Load-Balancing Networks
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Agarwal_2025, title={Invariant States of Hydrodynamic Limits of Randomized Load-Balancing Networks}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2020.0282}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2020.0282}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Agarwal, Pooja and Ramanan, Kavita}, year={2025}, month=june }
context:
Section 1.1 (Introduction), page 3.

Since the N-server stationary distributions are known to be tight (due to the results in [5]), to provide a rigorous proof of this convergence it would suffice to show that the solution to the hydrodynamic equations converges to the invariant state characterized here for a large class of initial conditions. Whereas this is a non-trivial problem that is relegated to future work, it is encouraging that analogous results have been recently established for a related problem.
title: Prove global convergence to the physical invariant state for hydrodynamic equations
background_latex:
Consider the SQ(d) randomized load-balancing model with a fixed integer choice parameter \(d\ge 2\). Jobs arrive to a system of \(N\) parallel single-server FCFS queues with total arrival rate \(\lambda N\), where \(\lambda\in(0,1)\). Each arriving job samples \(d\) servers uniformly at random (with replacement) and joins the shortest among the sampled queues (ties broken uniformly). Service times are i.i.d. with cumulative distribution function \(G\), density \(g\), and finite mean normalized to 1. Let \( ar G(x)=1-G(x)\), let \(L:=\sup\{x\ge 0:  ar G(x)>0\}\in(0,\infty]\) be the right endpoint of the support, and let the hazard rate be \(h(x)=g(x)/ ar G(x)\) for \(x\in[0,L)\).

In the hydrodynamic (mean-field) limit \(N	o\infty\), the system state at time \(t\ge 0\) is described by a countable collection of finite measures \(
u(t)=(
u_\ell(t))_{\ell\in\mathbb N}\), where \(
u_\ell(t)\) is a sub-probability measure on \([0,L)\) that places mass at the service ages of jobs that are currently in service at queues of length at least \(\ell\). The state space \(\mathcal S\) consists of sequences of sub-probability measures \((\mu_\ell)_{\ell\in\mathbb N}\) such that \(\langle f,\mu_\ellangle\ge \langle f,\mu_{\ell+1}angle\) for every nonnegative bounded continuous \(f\), where \(\langle f,\muangle:=\int f\,d\mu\).

Define for \(x\ge y\ge 0\)
\[P_d(x,y):= rac{x^d-y^d}{x-y}=\sum_{m=0}^{d-1} x^m y^{d-1-m}.\]
A trajectory \(
u(\cdot)\in C_{\mathcal S}[0,\infty)\) solves the hydrodynamic equations with arrival rate \(\lambda\) and initial condition \(
u(0)\in\mathcal S\) if it satisfies the coupled measure-valued integral equations (informally: mass balance plus transport/aging) given in the paper’s Definition 2.1; these involve the cumulative departure processes
\[D_\ell(t):=\int_0^t \langle h,
u_\ell(s)angle\,ds\]
and the routing measures \(\eta_\ell(t)\) defined from the queue-length tail fractions \(\langle 1,
u_\ell(t)angle\) via \(P_d\).

An invariant state is a fixed point \(
u^*\in\mathcal S\) such that the constant trajectory \(
u(t)\equiv 
u^*\) solves the hydrodynamic equations. Let \(s^*_\ell:=\langle 1,
u^*_\ellangle\) denote the invariant queue-length tail distribution (fraction of queues of length at least \(\ell\)). A "physical" invariant state additionally satisfies \(\lim_{\ell	o\infty} s^*_\ell=0\) (equivalently, has finite mean queue length in this tail sense).

For certain parameter regimes (e.g., \(d=2\) under the paper’s conditions), the paper establishes existence and/or uniqueness of the physical invariant state \(
u^*\) and provides a characterization of \(
u^*\) via fixed-point relations for associated densities \(r_\ell\) and the tail sequence \((s^*_\ell)\).
problem_definition_latex:
Fix \(\lambda\in(0,1)\), \(d\ge 2\), and a service-time distribution \(G\) satisfying the regularity conditions under which the hydrodynamic equations are well-posed and admit a (unique) physical invariant state \(
u^*\in\mathcal S\) with \(\lim_{\ell	o\infty} \langle 1,
u^*_\ellangle=0\).

Establish that, for a suitably large class of initial conditions \(
u(0)\in\mathcal S\) (as stated in the paper), the solution \(
u(t)\) to the hydrodynamic equations converges as \(t	o\infty\) to \(
u^*\) (in an appropriate topology on \(\mathcal S\)), i.e.
\[
u(t)\xrightarrow[t	o\infty]{}
u^*.\]
Equivalently, prove global convergence to equilibrium (asymptotic stability) of the hydrodynamic equations toward the physical invariant state.

open_problem_quote: Since the N-server stationary distributions are known to be tight (due to the results in [5]), to provide a rigorous proof of this convergence it would suffice to show that the solution to the hydrodynamic equations converges to the invariant state characterized here for a large class of initial conditions. Whereas this is a non-trivial problem that is relegated to future work, it is encouraging that analogous results have been recently established for a related problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is a global convergence theorem for an infinite-dimensional deterministic dynamical system; failure would require constructing a pathological initial condition and demonstrating non-convergence, which is not a simple finite combinatorial search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could simulate discretized versions of the hydrodynamic equations for many service distributions and initial conditions to look for Lyapunov candidates or to detect slow mixing/non-convergence numerically, but translating such evidence into a proof of global convergence is nontrivial and the fitness signal depends on accurate PDE/measure-valued simulation.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The hydrodynamic limit for SQ(d) with general service distribution \(G\) yields a countable family of coupled measure-valued transport equations for \(
u_\ell(t)\), with nonlinear boundary terms \(\eta_\ell(t)\) determined by the tail fractions \(s_\ell(t)=\langle 1,
u_\ell(t)angle\) through \(P_d\). Agarwal–Ramanan characterize physical invariant states \(
u^*\) via densities \(r_\ell\) satisfying the convolution-type recursion (their (3.3)–(3.4)), and prove uniqueness for \(d=2\) (Proposition 4.2). However, they leave open the global convergence problem: showing that for a broad class of initial conditions, the unique solution \(
u(t)\) converges as \(t	o\infty\) to the physical invariant state \(
u^*\).

Among forward citations provided, Atar–Kang–Kaspi–Ramanan develop general techniques to prove large-time convergence to equilibrium for a different but structurally related class of nonlinearly coupled measure-valued fluid equations (GI/G/N+G queues). Their approach supplies two potential blueprints for SQ(d): a renewal-equation reformulation enabling asymptotic estimates under monotone (e.g., decreasing) hazard rates, and a Lyapunov/relative-entropy method yielding strong convergence when the hazard rate is bounded away from 0 and \(\infty\). While these results do not directly address the SQ(d) coupling through \(P_d\) and the infinite-dimensional hierarchy in \(\ell\), they suggest that establishing a suitable Lyapunov functional or renewal-type representation adapted to the SQ(d) boundary terms could be a promising route.

At present, with the limited forward-citation set supplied, there is no paper that proves global convergence for the SQ(d) hydrodynamic equations with general \(G\). The problem therefore remains open; the main gap is to control the nonlinear, cross-level coupling \(\eta_\ell(t)\) and to propagate tail/compactness estimates uniformly in time so as to identify the \(\omega\)-limit set and rule out non-physical invariant states (e.g., the trivial \(s_\ell\equiv 1\) fixed point). Promising directions include constructing a monotone dynamical system comparison for the tail sequence \(s_\ell(t)\), developing a contractive metric on \(\mathcal S\) compatible with the transport structure, or adapting the relative-entropy Lyapunov strategy to the SQ(d) hierarchy by exploiting the explicit invariant-state densities \(r_\ell\) and the hazard-rate structure \(h=g/ ar G\).

sources.title: Long-time limit of nonlinearly coupled measure-valued equations that model many-server queues with reneging
sources.theorem_or_result:
For the GI/G/N+G many-server queue with reneging, the paper proves large-time convergence of the (deterministic) measure-valued fluid equations to the unique invariant state when \(\lambda
eq 1\) (and partial results at \(\lambda=1\) under decreasing hazard rate), under service-time hazard assumptions: either (i) \(h_s\) bounded away from 0 and \(\infty\), or (ii) \(h_s\) decreasing. In particular (Theorem 3.2): if \(\lambda<1\), then \((X_t,
u_t,\eta_t)	o (\lambda,\lambda
u^*,\lambda\eta^*)\); if \(\lambda>1\), then \(\eta_t\Rightarrow \lambda\eta^*\) and eventually \(B(t)=\langle 1,
u_tangle=1\) with \(
u_t\Rightarrow 
u^*\) and \(\langle h_s,
u_tangle	o 1\); moreover, under uniqueness of the invariant state, the Dirac mass at it is the unique random fixed point.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for global convergence to the physical invariant state for the hydrodynamic equations of the SQ(d) randomized load-balancing model with general service times. This paper does not study SQ(d); it studies a different mean-field/fluid model (many-server queue with reneging) governed by a coupled pair of measure-valued transport equations. However, it directly develops and executes two proof strategies for global convergence to a unique invariant state for measure-valued hydrodynamic equations: (a) a renewal-equation reformulation plus recursive asymptotic estimates when \(h_s\) is decreasing; (b) an extended relative-entropy Lyapunov functional yielding convergence (even in total variation) when \(h_s\) is bounded away from 0 and \(\infty\). The authors explicitly suggest these techniques may extend to load-balancing systems with general service distributions whose fluid limits are coupled measure-valued equations (citing Agarwal–Ramanan). Thus it provides relevant tools/blueprints but does not resolve convergence for the SQ(d) hydrodynamic equations.
